column6: Okay, then I can start off by saying that, basically, when a child or anyone comes across new information, we often think that we see something and immediately reactions come to our brain, right? But that's not the truth. First, you perceive it with your senses, so there's remembering what your senses have registered, and then understanding it. Initially, you understand it by getting some sort of basic idea. For example, if I look at a microphone, my basic senses tell me that it's silver, shiny, and smooth to touch. Then, we remember these things, and that's when higher-level cognition happens. For instance, you might evaluate it and think, 'This microphone is so shiny; maybe it's new or was gifted. How did the university manage to find such an expensive microphone?' At this point, you gather data and start to review or think about it in comparison to other microphones. For example, you might think, 'This is so expensive and new; maybe HKU has more funding than other universities.

column6: Yeah, but it's so much like last time when there were low-level and high-level years, which were more about interaction and learning. Cognitive affective cognition is related to the brain, and affective is more related to our emotional states. The idea is that when students discuss anything in class, they have their own opinions, emotions, and ideas. Depending on that, they can either be low-level engagers or high-level engagers. If someone is a low-level passive engager, they are listening more and then evaluating and analyzing what other people are saying, whereas voices are obviously being more active participants. Within passive and active engagement, there are subcategories, if I'm not mistaken. With interactive engagement, the idea is that we get readings. I don't know what majors you guys do, but you get readings, and then interactive cognitive processing is based on the assumption that all students have already done the reading. They come with this knowledge base and are just sharing their affective processing of the situation. Everyone is sharing their subjective affective processing because they have already done their more objective cognitive processing at home. In constructive engagement or classroom engagement or learning, you are essentially deconstructing your set ideas about something. You are evaluating both your cognitive and affective, meaning both your emotions and your analytical understanding of something. For example, if you go to a history class, you might come in with the mindset that too much immigration is bad, or someone might come in with a hardline opinion, like every single immigrant in Hong Kong should speak Japanese. Then, you'll be asked to constructively engage and think about why you hold these opinions and beliefs. You might even be pushed to a much deeper level than you expected, such as questioning whether countries should even have national languages, what it means to have a national language, what it means to have immigrants, and to what extent immigrants should integrate. So, essentially, passive learning is the least form of engagement you can have with a concept, but then there is more interactive engagement, and finally, more constructive engagement is what you want because you want students to deconstruct and reconstruct their ideas.

column5: blooms taxonomy is basically its sort of an educational learning model which is used for classifying cognitive processes into a hierarchical order, so it starts off with remembering, which is like simple recalling and recalling information from memory, and then there is understanding where you understand the i or comprehend the idea in a much more deeper sense, then theres application where you would use your knowledge to solve problems, and then theres analyzing where you would differentiate or organize different concepts and break it into different fragments, and then lastly, would be evaluating that is like judging the quality of the of the concept or evaluating the criticality of the concept, perhaps and then lastly, create where you design or construct your own ideas,
column6: Bloom's Taxonomy is essentially an educational learning model used to classify cognitive processes in a hierarchical order. It begins with remembering, which involves simple recalling and retrieving information from memory. Next is understanding, where one comprehends or grasps the idea on a deeper level. Following that is application, which involves using knowledge to solve problems. Then comes analyzing, where one differentiates, organizes, and breaks down concepts into smaller fragments. After that, there is evaluating, which entails judging the quality or critically assessing the concept. Finally, there is creating, where one designs or constructs their own ideas.
